system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 4: Module 3 - Painting by Numbers and Arrangement Examples

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 4 — Module 3 - Painting by Numbers and Arrangement Examples

_Pages 112-136._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Where to place chords

> This final step is a straightforward process of applying a chord to the first melody note of each bar
> and/or chord change in the lead sheet.

**Significance (p. 113).** Concrete placement rule for the painting-by-numbers method — chord on bar 1 or chord-change beat.

## Painting by numbers rule

> Choose chord voicings that have the same interval of the melody note as the highest note in the
>  chord shape.

**Significance (p. 114).** The single load-bearing voicing-selection rule that the whole method depends on.

## Adapt voicing for melody

> a voicing that can be slightly adapted to have the melody note as the highest note and still sound
> good.

**Significance (p. 114).** Carve-out from the strict rule: voicings can be tweaked rather than always matched exactly.

## Three voicing-lookup inputs

> To know which voicing to pick we need to establish:
>
> 1. The string the melody note is on
> 2. The type of chord that is in the chord progression whilst that melody note is played
> 3. The interval of the melody note in relation to that chord.

**Significance (p. 114).** Defines the three index keys for the chord-dictionary lookup.

## Transpose by sliding shape

> Easily fixed – just shift the shape down on the fretboard so that the top note matches the same fret
> where the melody note is on our score:

**Significance (p. 118).** Operational rule for adapting a dictionary voicing into the key of the tune at hand.

## Less is more

> In fact, less is more – placing chords at key places ‘here and there’ is easier on the hands and is
> usually more than enough to imply the harmony beneath the melody line. So don’t get carried away!

**Significance (p. 122).** Pedagogical rule against overharmonization, with the implied-harmony rationale.

## Playability over ambition

> “If it’s easy on the hands, the music will flow better – so most of the time making things as easy
>  as possible is musically the best option anyway”.

**Significance (p. 124).** Cited rule that justifies cutting voicings to improve playability — the key principle behind the refine step.

## Three options for hard passages

> We have some options here to make this passage more playable:
>
> 1. Cut out notes from the chord – e.g. we could cut that low B to make it easier to change to the
> next chord:

**Significance (p. 125).** Names the first of three remediation strategies for awkward voicings.

## Diads/octaves alternative

> 2. Choose a different harmonization approach – e.g. instead of block chords I could use parallel
> octaves or diads (a “chord” with only two notes). Here’s an example of diads in 3rds:

**Significance (p. 125).** Second remediation strategy and definition of diads in the method.

## Removing chord entirely

> Removing the difficult voicings completely often makes the whole passage work much better on the
> guitar – it helps my hands to relax.

**Significance (p. 126).** Third remediation strategy — author's most-used escape valve for awkward voicings.

## Tension from dominants

> The jazzy sound in arrangements usually comes from adding extensions and tensions to voicings –
> particularly to dominant chords.

**Significance (p. 127).** Rule of thumb: where the 'jazzy' quality lives and which chord type to target for adding tensions.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
